title: How to Talk to the Part of You That Won't Stop Worrying
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Learn to understand the part of you that won't stop worrying through the lens of Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation. Discover how to build a new relationship with your anxiety.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently hold a luminous, golden thread, weaving it into a complex, glowing tapestry. The background is soft and warm, suggesting a process of gentle repair and integration. The overall feeling is one of hope, healing, and the quiet beauty of mending what was broken.
finalH2: From Ceaseless Vigilance to Embodied Presence
htmlContent:
Janina Fisher, a pivotal voice in the landscape of trauma recovery, spent decades mapping the terrain of the fragmented self, offering a profound insight that the parts of us we often fight against are not enemies, but architects of survival. The theory of structural dissociation she champions suggests that the incessant worrier inside—the one that rehearses every possible negative outcome and scans the horizon for threats—is not a malfunction, but a highly sophisticated, protective mechanism born from necessity. This part, which Fisher might term an "Apparently Normal Part" or ANP, took on the immense job of navigating the complexities of daily life while other parts of the system held the raw, un-metabolized experiences of trauma. Understanding this is the first step toward a different kind of internal conversation, one that moves beyond frustration and into a space of compassionate inquiry. What if that worrying part isn't the problem, but a guide trying to lead us back to a sense of wholeness? To begin this journey, it's essential to first understand what complex trauma is and how it shapes our internal world.

The Unseen Architect of Your Worry

The constant hum of worry that many of us experience is not a personal failing or a sign of a broken mind; it is the diligent, tireless work of a part of the self that was created to keep us safe. In her work on structural dissociation, Janina Fisher explains that in the face of overwhelming experiences, particularly in childhood, the personality can fragment into different parts, each with its own distinct job. There are the parts that hold the trauma—the Emotional Parts (EPs)—and then there is the part that gets on with the business of living, the ANP. This ANP is the one that goes to work, pays the bills, and maintains relationships, all while carrying the immense burden of keeping the trauma-related parts out of conscious awareness. The worry we feel is often the sound of this ANP working overtime, a constant and exhausting vigilance that it believes is necessary for survival. It’s a profound reframe to consider that this worrying part is not trying to make our lives miserable, but is, in fact, desperately trying to protect us from ever feeling that level of helplessness and pain again. It’s a guardian, a protector, a manager—and it’s been doing its job with unwavering dedication for a very long time. This part carries the cognitive and emotional load of our daily existence, and its worrying is a testament to its profound commitment to our survival. It is the part that remembers the chaos and unpredictability of the past and has vowed to never let us be caught off guard again. Its methods may be exhausting, but its motives are pure. To begin to heal is to begin to recognize this part not as a source of pathology, but as a source of profound strength and resilience.

Why the Nervous System Built a Worrier-in-Chief

Our nervous system is a marvel of efficiency, designed to learn from the past to predict the future, all in the service of keeping us alive. When the past is characterized by chronic threat, the nervous system adapts by becoming a master of threat detection. It learns that the world is not a safe place, and it wires itself for hypervigilance. The shift happens when the worrier-in-chief is born. It is the cognitive extension of a nervous system that has concluded, based on overwhelming evidence, that danger is always just around the corner. This part of us becomes an expert at running simulations of what could go wrong, not out of a morbid fascination with disaster, but as a desperate attempt to be prepared for it. It operates on the flawed but understandable logic that if it can just anticipate every possible threat, it can prevent us from being blindsided again. It doesn’t understand that the war is over; it’s still on the battlefield, scanning the horizon for enemy combatants. It is a biological imperative, a survival strategy that has outlived its usefulness but doesn’t know how to stand down. The neural pathways that support this hypervigilance become deeply entrenched, creating a default mode of functioning that is characterized by anxiety and worry. The amygdala, the brain’s smoke detector, becomes hypersensitive, sounding the alarm at the slightest hint of danger, real or imagined. The prefrontal cortex, the part of the brain responsible for rational thought and impulse control, is often sidelined in these moments, leaving the more primitive, survival-oriented parts of the brain in charge. This is not a choice; it is a physiological reality. The worrier-in-chief is not a psychological quirk; it is a neurobiological adaptation to an environment that was fundamentally unsafe.

The brain is prediction machinery. Anxiety is just prediction running without a stop button.

The Futility of Reassurance and the Language of Sensation

One of the most frustrating aspects of living with a relentless inner worrier is the utter failure of logic to soothe it. We can tell ourselves a thousand times that everything is okay, that our fears are irrational, that we are safe now, but the worrying persists, unabated. This is because the worrying part does not speak the language of logic; it speaks the language of sensation. It is a creature of the body, not the mind. It communicates in the felt sense of a tight chest, a clenched jaw, a racing heart. These are the signals that it sends and receives, the currency of its world. To try and reason with it is like trying to teach a fish to climb a tree. It’s a category error. The work, then, is not to argue with the worrier, but to learn its language. It is to drop out of the story and into the body, to meet the sensations of fear and anxiety with a curious and compassionate attention. It is to understand that the body remembers what the mind cannot, and that true safety is not a thought, but a felt experience. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals try to out-think their anxiety, only to find themselves more entangled in it. The path out is not through the head, but through the heart, and the body is the doorway. This means learning to notice the subtle shifts in our physiology, the tightening in our shoulders, the heat in our face, the shallowness of our breath. These are not random occurrences; they are communications from the parts of us that are holding fear. To learn their language is to learn the language of the body, a language that is spoken in the present moment, a language that is always true.

The contemplative traditions all point to the same thing: what you're looking for is what's looking.

Dual Awareness: The Practice of Witnessing Without Fusing

Janina Fisher’s concept of “dual awareness” offers a powerful practice for working with these fragmented parts of the self. It is the ability to hold two streams of awareness simultaneously: the awareness of the present moment, and the awareness of the part’s experience. It is the capacity to be both in the here and now—feeling the ground beneath our feet, the air on our skin—and to acknowledge the reality of the part that is trapped in the there and then, reliving the fears and pains of the past. This is not a state of being half-in, half-out, but a radical act of inclusion. It is the adult self saying to the child part, “I see you. I feel you. I know you are scared. And I am here with you, in this present moment, where we are safe.” A client once described this as learning to be the container for their own experience, rather than being consumed by it. It is the difference between being the storm and being the sky that holds the storm. This practice of dual awareness is the foundation of a new internal relationship, one that is built on presence, compassion, and the slow, steady cultivation of trust. It is a practice that we can cultivate in small moments throughout our day. When we feel a wave of anxiety, we can pause, take a breath, and acknowledge the presence of the worrying part. We can feel our feet on the floor, notice the colors in the room, and at the same time, extend a sense of kindness and curiosity to the part of us that is struggling. This is not about pushing the fear away, but about making space for it, holding it in a larger container of awareness. It is a practice of both/and, a practice of radical acceptance, a practice that slowly, over time, rewires our nervous system for safety and connection.

A New Internal Dialogue: Speaking to the Guardian

Engaging with the worrying part requires a new kind of internal dialogue, one that is rooted in respect and curiosity rather than judgment and frustration. The first step is simply to acknowledge its presence. To turn toward it and say, “I see you. I know you are here.” The second step is to validate its protective intention. To say, “I know you are working so hard to keep me safe. Thank you for your tireless vigilance.” The third step is to gently inquire about its fears. To ask, “What are you so afraid will happen right now?” And the fourth step is to offer safety from the perspective of the adult self. To say, “I am here now. I am grown. I can handle this. You don’t have to carry this burden alone anymore.” This is not a one-time conversation, but an ongoing practice of turning toward, listening to, and reassuring the parts of us that have been carrying so much for so long. It is a way of reparenting ourselves, of offering the comfort and safety that was not available in the past. As we do this, we begin to build a new internal architecture, one that is based on collaboration rather than conflict. We learn to navigate the complexities of life not as a fractured and warring internal system, but as an integrated and compassionate whole. This is the path to navigating emotional flashbacks and building a life that is not defined by the past. It is a journey that requires us to understand the four F trauma responses and how they show up in our daily lives. This new dialogue is not about placating the part, but about genuinely connecting with it. It is about seeing it, hearing it, and understanding its world. It is about building a bridge of communication between the adult self and the child parts, a bridge that is built on empathy, respect, and a shared desire for healing.

Weighted blankets provide deep pressure stimulation that can help regulate the nervous system. A popular choice is a weighted blanket (paid link).

If your spiritual practice makes you more rigid, it's not working.

The Slow Architecture of Internal Trust

Building a relationship with the parts of ourselves that have been exiled and maligned is not a quick fix. It is the slow, patient work of building trust where there has been none. It is a process of showing up, again and again, with a compassionate and non-judgmental presence. It is about demonstrating, through our actions, that we are a safe harbor for all of our parts, even the ones that we have been taught to fear and reject. This is not a process of eliminating the worrying part, but of integrating it. It is about helping it to see that it no longer needs to be on high alert, that it can relax its vigilance, that it can trust the adult self to navigate the world. This is a profound and often challenging journey, one that requires a deep commitment to our own healing. But it is also a journey that is rich with rewards. As we begin to build this internal trust, we find that the constant hum of worry begins to quiet down. We find that we have more access to our creativity, our joy, our aliveness. We begin to experience a sense of wholeness that we may have never thought possible, a state that is often described in the context of Self-energy in IFS. This process is not linear. There will be days when the worrying part is loud and insistent, and days when it is quiet and distant. The key is to remain steady in our commitment to this new relationship, to continue to show up with kindness and curiosity, to continue to offer the safety and reassurance that was so painfully absent in the past. This is the slow, steady work of re-weaving the fabric of the self, of creating a new internal tapestry that is strong, resilient, and whole.

The most important things in life cannot be understood — only experienced.

From Ceaseless Vigilance to Embodied Presence

The ultimate goal of this work is not to get rid of the worrying part, but to help it to transform. It is to help it to see that its energy, its intelligence, its vigilance can be used in the service of a different kind of life—a life that is not defined by fear, but by presence. As we build a relationship with this part, as we offer it the safety and compassion that it has been so desperately craving, it begins to relax. Its ceaseless vigilance gives way to a more embodied and discerning awareness. It learns to differentiate between real threats and the echoes of past trauma. It begins to trust that the adult self is capable of handling the challenges of life. This is not a linear process, and there will be times when the worrying part will become activated again. But with practice, we can learn to meet these moments with a sense of curiosity and compassion, rather than with fear and frustration. We can learn to see them as opportunities to deepen our relationship with this part of ourselves, to offer it more of the safety and reassurance that it needs. This is the journey from a life of ceaseless vigilance to a life of embodied presence, a life in which we are no longer at war with ourselves, but are instead a compassionate and integrated whole. This transformation is not a destination, but a process, a dance between the past and the present, between the parts and the whole. It is a journey of coming home to ourselves, of reclaiming the parts of us that have been lost in the shadows, of finally, finally, learning to befriend the beautiful, broken, and resilient beings that we are.

So what are you going to do about it? Not tomorrow. Not after you finish reading the next article. Right now. The body is waiting for your answer.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is the worrying part the same as my anxiety?

While they are deeply interconnected, it can be helpful to see the worrying part as the *source* of the anxiety. The part is the entity, the sub-personality, that is generating the anxious thoughts and feelings as a protective strategy. The anxiety is the emotional and physiological output of that part's activity. By relating to the part itself, we can get to the root of the anxiety, rather than just trying to manage the symptoms.

Will this practice get rid of my worrying part forever?

The goal is not to eliminate any part of yourself, but to integrate it. The worrying part has valuable qualities—it is vigilant, strategic, and deeply committed to your survival. The aim is to help it relax its hypervigilance and to lend its strengths to your adult self, so that it can become a source of wisdom and discernment rather than a source of chronic anxiety. It’s a job change, not a termination.

What if I can't feel or hear any parts?

This is very common. For many, the concept of "parts" is purely intellectual at first. The key is to start with the felt sense in the body. When you feel that familiar knot of worry in your stomach, you can simply acknowledge, "Ah, the worrying part is here." You don't need to have a full-blown conversation. The simple act of acknowledging its presence with curiosity is the beginning of the relationship.

What becomes possible when the energy we spend fighting ourselves is finally liberated for the work of living?

The question is not whether you are ready. It is whether you are willing.
